content:
9 min read
Just now

--

“Have you mostly worked in a large existing codebase, or on greenfield projects?”
The recruiter asked me this twice on the same call, worded two different ways. I only noticed the second time. My guess is she wanted to see if my answer stayed the same both times, since nobody at a company the size of PayPal hands a new hire a blank project.
Friend link:
I told her about a messy codebase I once inherited — half old Objective-C, half newer Swift, barely any tests on the old half. I talked about how I made changes there without breaking things I didn’t fully understand yet. That landed a lot better than the first thing that almost came out of my mouth, which was something close to “I’m comfortable with legacy code,” a line so generic it means nothing.
Two problems, ninety minutes, and one that tricked me
The online assessment had two coding problems.
The first one: group a list of transactions by category, add up the totals, keep it fast. A hash map handles this in your sleep. Done in a few minutes, forgot about it right after.
The second one looked scary until it wasn’t. You get a table of currency exchange rates — dollars to euros, euros to yen, that kind of thing — and you have to figure out if there’s a chain of trades that makes you free money out of nothing. That’s called arbitrage.
